source_texts: Of course, I hated them all in our office, from first to last, and despised them all, but at the same time I was also as if afraid of them. It happened that I would suddenly set them above myself. Things were somehow sudden with me in those days: now I despised them, now I set them above me. A developed and decent man cannot be vain without a boundless exactingness towards himself and without despising himself at moments to the point of hatred. But whether I despised them or set them above me, I used to drop my eyes before almost everyone I met. I even made experiments: will I be able to endure so-and-so's glance on me? – and I was always the first to drop my eyes. This tormented me to the point of fury. I was also afraid to the point of illness of being ridiculous, and therefore slavishly worshiped routine in everything to do with externals; I loved falling into the common rut, and feared any eccentricity in myself with all my soul. But how could I hold out? I was morbidly developed, as a man of our time ought to be developed. And they were all dull-witted and as like one another as a flock of sheep. Perhaps to me alone in the whole office did it constantly seem that I was a coward and a slave; it seemed so to me precisely because I was developed. But it not only seemed, in fact it really was so: I was a coward and a slave. I say it without any embarrassment. Every decent man of our time is and must be a coward and a slave. That is his normal condition. I am deeply convinced of it. He's made that way, and arranged for it. And not in the present time, owing to some sort of chance circumstances, but generally in all times a decent man must be a coward and a slave. That is the natural law of all decent people on earth. If one of them does happen to get up a bit of pluck in something, let him not be eased or pleased by that: he'll still quail before something else. Such is the sole and everlasting outcome. Only asses and their mongrels show pluck, and even then only up to that certain wall. It's not worth paying any attention to them, because they mean precisely nothing.

This was the philosophy worked out by the Oblomovka Plato, a philosophy which lulled him amid the issues and strict demands of duty and purpose! He had been born and reared not like a gladiator, for the arena, but as a peaceful spectator of the battle. It was not for his meek and lazy soul to endure either the alarms of happiness or life’s blows. Consequently, his person expressed one of life’s extremes, and he did nothing to achieve, change, or repent.
“For me the Prince happens to be the first person in my life in whom I recognized a truly loyal person. He believed in me from the first, and I believe in him.”
Nikolai Apollonovich perceived a shady type (perhaps of the petty bourgeoisie) with his neck wrapped up.

The torso of a capripede caryatid. A carriage drawn by a pair of steeds flew up to the carriage porch, and a lackey, tricorne on his head, gave the handle a click, and the door flew open, flinging back a coat of arms adorned with tiny crowns (a unicorn goring a knight). Responding to greetings, a hand invested in leather touched the brim of a top hat.

The truth is that no one has ever really explained how Joseph Buquet met his death. The verdict at the inquest was ‘suicide under natural circumstances’. In his Memoirs of a Theatre Director, Armand Moncharmin, one of the co-directors who succeeded Messrs Debienne and Poligny, relates the incident as follows: A most unfortunate accident ruined the little party that Messrs Debienne and Poligny were giving to mark their departure.
Which is why, we all agreed to crown Sundara Chola, Gandaradithar’s nephew as the next king. And Sundara Chola has been a good King to all of us, until two years ago when he fell ill.

“Well, I’d rather put it this way: you and I, we have the same philosophy. That would be a much fairer statement. Je pense, donc je suis, that is something I know for certain. As for the rest—all those worlds, God, and even Satan himself—I’m not sure whether all that exists independently or is merely a passing and subjective emanation of myself .
‘How can you doubt it! But you, who do not love her, why are you so ready to endanger your life? It must be that you hate Erik.’

And the zemstvo official stopped in perplexity, clutching at a tuft of his beard. The lonely domino begged not to be driven out into the Petersburg slush, begged not to be driven out into the malignant fog. The zemstvo official apparently wanted to make a little joke, and grunted: “Mm . . .
rationales: Outputs 2 and 4 share very distinctive and consistent phrasing patterns: both use 'drop/dropped my eyes,' 'slavishly observed/worshiped routine in everything external,' 'falling into the common rut,' 'feared any eccentricity,' and nearly identical structures in the 'coward and a slave' section. The word choices such as 'tormented/drove me to frenzy/fury' and the overall sentence-level structure align closely between these two, suggesting they originate from the same paraphrasing system. Output 1, while similar, contains an unusual repetition artifact that sets it apart, and Outputs 0 and 3 are clearly more freely rewritten.

Outputs 3 and 4 share nearly identical vocabulary and phrasing throughout: 'gladiator, for the arena,' 'peaceful spectator of the battle,' 'meek and lazy soul,' 'blows of happiness,' 'achieve, change, or repent,' 'Oblomovka Plato,' and 'strict demands of duty and purpose.' The differences are minor (slight sentence reordering, 'shocks' vs 'blows' of happiness), consistent with two outputs from the same paraphraser producing near-literal renditions with small variations.

Outputs 0 and 3 share distinctive stylistic patterns: both use the phrase 'reciprocate/reciprocated that trust,' both employ formal restructuring of the original idea, and both use similar vocabulary ('truly,' loyalty-related terms). The shared construction around 'reciprocating trust' is a strong fingerprint, as none of the other outputs use this phrasing. Both also avoid the more literal renderings seen in outputs 2 and 4.

Outputs 0 and 3 share nearly identical phrasing: both begin with 'Nikolai Apollonovich noticed a suspicious-looking individual, possibly from the lower middle class,' and differ only in the final clause describing the neck covering. This level of structural and lexical overlap strongly suggests they come from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 3 share almost identical sentence structures and word choices: both begin with 'A carriage drawn by a pair of steeds flew up to the [porte-cochère/porch]', both describe 'gave a/the click to the handle, and the door flew open', both use 'adorned with little crowns (a unicorn goring a knight)', both use 'a hand in leather touched the brim of a top hat', and both end with 'The torso of a capripede caryatid/capripede-caryatid'. These close parallels in vocabulary, phrasing, and structure indicate they were produced by the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 3 share the most consistent stylistic fingerprints: both use the word 'marred' to describe the party incident, both refer to a 'farewell party/celebration,' both paraphrase Moncharmin's account without using direct quotation marks for his words, both describe the suicide ruling with a similar structure ('under normal/ordinary circumstances'), and both adopt a flowing narrative style that avoids quoting the book title in the same formal way as outputs 1 and 4. These shared patterns strongly suggest they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 2 and 3 share the same structural pattern: both begin with the collective decision to install Sundara Chola (using 'collectively decided'), then describe his effective/benevolent rule, and conclude with his illness two years ago. They also both correctly order events (decision first, then reign, then illness), use similar formal tone, and mirror each other's sentence organization closely, suggesting they came from the same paraphraser.

Outputs 0 and 4 share distinctive stylistic fingerprints: both fully translate 'Je pense, donc je suis' into English rather than keeping the French, both use 'absolutely' as an intensifier ('absolutely sure of' / 'absolutely convinced of'), both frame the uncertainty about worlds/God/Satan with very similar phrasing ('not certain/not convinced whether they're real/exist independently or just a product/personal reflections of my own mind'), and both include a meta-reframing introduction ('I prefer to phrase it'/'Let me rephrase it'). These parallel choices strongly suggest the same paraphraser.

Outputs 3 and 4 share the same structural pattern (short rhetorical opener → 'no affection for her' phrase → 'It must be...' conclusion about Erik), use nearly identical phrasing including 'no affection for her' and the 'It must be' construction, and both maintain a compact, declarative style without dialogue tags or extra conversational elements.

Outputs 3 and 4 share nearly identical sentence structures, narrative detail, and linguistic patterns: both use 'stood still in perplexity, clutching/grasping a tuft of his beard in one hand,' both repeat the pleading phrase twice ('begging/imploring him not to drive him/it from this house back into the Petersburg slush... back into the [cruel and dense/dense, malevolent] fog'), both note the official 'evidently wanted/had it in mind to make a joke,' both use a throat-clearing phrase ('hemmed and hawed'/'cleared his throat'), and both conclude with 'the joke assumed a... incoherent form.' This level of structural and phraseological parallelism strongly indicates they came from the same paraphraser.

human_par3: Needless to say, I hated all the members of my department from the first to the last, and despised them, and yet somehow feared them as well. At times I would even rate them above myself. This quite often happened to me at that time; at one moment I despised them, at the next I felt they were superior to me. A decent and intelligent man cannot be vain without making inordinate demands on himself and at times despising himself to the point of hatred. But whether I despised them or rated them above myself, I dropped my eyes before almost everybody I came across. I even tried experiments: ‘Shall I be able to bear so-and-so’s eyes upon me?’ – and I was always the first to lower my eyes. This drove me to frenzy. I was also morbidly afraid of being ridiculous and therefore slavishly observed the ordinary conventions in every outward appearance; I enthusiastically followed the common rut and was terrified of developing any eccentricity. But how was I to keep it up? I was painfully advanced, as a man of our time should be. But they were dullards, as like one another as a flock of sheep. Perhaps I was the only one in the whole office who always seemed to myself a slave and a coward precisely because I also seemed (to myself) civilized. But I not only seemed, I really was a coward and a slave. I say this without shame. Every decent man in this age is, and must be, a coward and a slave. That is his normal condition. I am profoundly certain of this. That is how he is made and what he was made for. And not only at the present time, because of what may be accidental circumstances, but at all times, a decent man must be a coward and a slave. This is a law of the nature of all decent people on the earth. Even if one of them puts on a show of bravery before somebody else, he ought not to take comfort from that or let himself be carried away by it: he is only showing off to the other person. This is the only and the eternal solution. Only donkeys and mules make a show of bravery, and they only to a limited extent. It is not worth while paying any attention to them, because they count for exactly nothing.
llama_31_8b: I had a complex and often contradictory relationship with my coworkers in the office. On the one hand, I hated them all and felt a strong sense of disdain, but on the other hand, I was also intimidated by them. This led to a pattern of vacillating between feeling superior and inferior to them. I was particularly prone to feelings of self-directed contempt, which I believe is a natural part of being a well-adjusted and critically thinking person. However, this tendency to be overly self-critical would often manifest in a fear of being ridiculed, leading me to internalize a rigid set of social norms and stick to what was considered "normal" and "acceptable". Despite my surface-level appearance of confidence and composure, I was actually deeply anxious and fearful of not meeting societal expectations. In reality, I was both a coward and a slave to the conventions of the time, and this was not just a temporary state, but a fundamental aspect of being a decent person. I genuinely believe that this is the natural condition of anyone who values decency, that is, being constantly intimidated and fearful of making mistakes or being judged by others. This dynamic is an integral part of the human experience across all time periods, and it's only those who are naive or unrefined who would suggest otherwise.
gemini_25_flash:
I detested everyone in my office, from the highest to the lowest, and looked down on them, yet paradoxically, I also felt intimidated by them. My feelings were often extreme back then: one moment I'd scorn them, the next I'd elevate them in my mind. A sophisticated and respectable person cannot be arrogant without also being incredibly self-critical and, at times, loathing themselves. Regardless of whether I held them in contempt or respect, I would invariably avoid eye contact with almost everyone I encountered. I even tried to withstand someone's gaze, but I always looked away first, which infuriated me. I had an overwhelming fear of appearing foolish, so I slavishly adhered to conventional practices in all outward matters. I enjoyed conforming and dreaded any personal eccentricity.

However, how could I truly conform? I was excessively intellectual, as a modern person should be, while they were all simple-minded and indistinguishable from each other. Perhaps I was the only one in the office who constantly perceived myself as a coward and a subservient individual, and this perception stemmed directly from my intellectual development. But it wasn't just a feeling; it was the truth: I was a coward and a slave. I state this without shame. In our era, every respectable person is inherently a coward and a slave. This is their natural state, and I am profoundly convinced of it. This isn't due to temporary circumstances, but rather, a fundamental characteristic of respectable individuals throughout history. This is the inherent law for all decent people on Earth. If one of them manages to show a brief moment of courage, they shouldn't take comfort in it, as they will inevitably falter in the face of something else. This is the consistent and inevitable outcome. Only fools and their mixed breeds display courage, and even then, only up to a certain limit. They are not worth considering, as they hold no true significance.

human_par3: Such was the philosophy that the Plato of Oblomovka had worked out and that lulled him to sleep amidst the stern demands of duty and the problems of human existence! He was not born and educated to be a gladiator for the arena, but a peaceful spectator of the battle; his timid and indolent spirit could not have endured either the anxieties of happiness or the blows inflicted by life – therefore he merely gave expression to one particular aspect of it, and it was no use being sorry or trying to change it or to get more out of it.
llama_31_8b: The Oblomovka philosopher lived according to a unique set of principles, allowing him to remain detached amidst life's demands and the pressure of responsibility. This approach was shaped by his upbringing and personality, which made him more suited to observing life's events from a safe distance, rather than engaging with the challenges and risks that came with it. As someone with a laid-back and gentle nature, he was not wired to face the intense highs and lows of life, and as a result, he existed in a state of apathy, neither striving to achieve, change, nor show remorse.
gemini_25_flash: The perspective developed by the 'Oblomovka Plato' was one that kept him comfortable, shielding him from the pressing obligations and challenges of life. He wasn't raised to be a fighter in life's struggles, but rather an observer from the sidelines. His gentle and passive nature couldn't handle the excitement of joy or the hardships of existence. As a result, his character represented an ultimate state of inactivity; he made no effort to accomplish anything, initiate change, or express regret.
dipper_lowmed: He was born and brought up not like a gladiator, for the arena, but as a peaceful spectator of the battle; neither the blows of happiness, nor the blows of life were for his meek and lazy soul. Consequently, his person expressed one extreme of life, and he did not do anything to achieve, change or repent. This philosophy of Oblomov's Plato, which lulled him amid the clashes and strict demands of duty and purpose, was the philosophy of Plato.

It was he who had been born and brought up, not as a gladiator, for the arena, but as a peaceful spectator of the battle; it was not for his meek and lazy soul to endure the shocks of happiness and the blows of life; consequently, his person expressed one of the extremes of life, and he did not achieve, change, or repent. This was the philosophy of Oblomovka Plato, a philosophy that lulled him in the midst of the problems and strict demands of duty and purpose!

dipper_lowmed: “The prince happens to be the first person in my life whom I recognized as a truly loyal man. He believed in me from the very beginning and I believe in him.”

human_par3: And—the torso of a goat-footed caryatid. Since such time as a carriage drawn by a pair of lathery black horses came hurtling up to the entrance-porch, since such time as a court footman in a loose-flowing cape with a tricorn askew on his head first threw open the lacquered, emblazoned orifice, and the door, with a click, cast aside a coat of arms (a unicorn, striking a knight) embellished with crowns; since such time as a statue with a face of parchment, rising from the coach’s funereal cushions, set foot upon the granite of the entrance-porch; since such time as a suede-gloved hand touched the rim of a top hat as it returned other people’s bows:—since such time a yet more mighty power had subdued the Establishment which cast its own mighty power all across Russia.

human_par3: Unfortunately, cruel destiny did not allow Arinjaya to rule our land for more than a year; his son Paranthaka Sundara Chozhar was a strapping young man of twenty years. We, the Empire's pillars, the ministers, aides, chieftains, lords, kottam and kootram officials gathered, discussed the best interests of the country, and crowned Sundara Chozhar as King. There is no doubt that this was an excellent decision; until two years ago, Sundara Chozhar gave us no cause to doubt his rule.

human_par3: "Well, if you like, I have the same philosophy as you, that would be true. _Je pense, donc je suis_, I know that for a fact; all the rest, all these worlds, God and even Satan--all that is not proved, to my mind. Does all that exist of itself, or is it only an emanation of myself, a logical development of my ego which alone has existed for ever: but I make haste to stop, for I believe you will be jumping up to beat me directly."
llama_31_8b: I'd put it more simply: You and I, our views align. It would be more accurate to say that. I think, therefore I exist, that is one truth I'm certain of. As for the rest, the realm of the gods, eternal beings, and even the concept of the devil - I remain uncertain whether they are objective realities or just fleeting manifestations of my own mind.

"For clarity, I'd say that we share the same basic outlook. Saying we do would be more accurate. The idea that I think, therefore I am is something I'm absolutely convinced of. As for the rest - the various worlds, God, and even the concept of the devil - I'm not convinced whether they're real entities in their own right or just a product of my own subjective thoughts and feelings."

I detested everyone in my office, from the highest to the lowest, and looked down on them, yet paradoxically, I also felt intimidated by them. My feelings were often extreme back then: one moment I'd scorn them, the next I'd elevate them in my mind. A sophisticated and respectable person cannot be arrogant without also being incredibly self-critical and, at times, loathing themselves. Regardless of whether I held them in contempt or respect, I would invariably avoid eye contact with almost everyone I encountered. I even tried to withstand someone's gaze, but I always looked away first, which infuriated me. I had an overwhelming fear of appearing foolish, so I slavishly adhered to conventional practices in all outward matters. I enjoyed conforming and dreaded any personal eccentricity.

However, how could I truly conform? I was excessively intellectual, as a modern person should be, while they were all simple-minded and indistinguishable from each other. Perhaps I was the only one in the office who constantly perceived myself as a coward and a subservient individual, and this perception stemmed directly from my intellectual development. But it wasn't just a feeling; it was the truth: I was a coward and a slave. I state this without shame. In our era, every respectable person is inherently a coward and a slave. This is their natural state, and I am profoundly convinced of it. This isn't due to temporary circumstances, but rather, a fundamental characteristic of respectable individuals throughout history. This is the inherent law for all decent people on Earth. If one of them manages to show a brief moment of courage, they shouldn't take comfort in it, as they will inevitably falter in the face of something else. This is the consistent and inevitable outcome. Only fools and their mixed breeds display courage, and even then, only up to a certain limit. They are not worth considering, as they hold no true significance.

The Oblomovka philosopher lived according to a unique set of principles, allowing him to remain detached amidst life's demands and the pressure of responsibility. This approach was shaped by his upbringing and personality, which made him more suited to observing life's events from a safe distance, rather than engaging with the challenges and risks that came with it. As someone with a laid-back and gentle nature, he was not wired to face the intense highs and lows of life, and as a result, he existed in a state of apathy, neither striving to achieve, change, nor show remorse.
"The Prince is, in my experience, the only truly steadfast individual I've encountered. His belief in me was immediate, and I reciprocate that trust."

Such was the philosophy that the Plato of Oblomovka had worked out and that lulled him to sleep amidst the stern demands of duty and the problems of human existence! He was not born and educated to be a gladiator for the arena, but a peaceful spectator of the battle; his timid and indolent spirit could not have endured either the anxieties of happiness or the blows inflicted by life – therefore he merely gave expression to one particular aspect of it, and it was no use being sorry or trying to change it or to get more out of it.

Needless to say, I hated all the members of my department from the first to the last, and despised them, and yet somehow feared them as well. At times I would even rate them above myself. This quite often happened to me at that time; at one moment I despised them, at the next I felt they were superior to me. A decent and intelligent man cannot be vain without making inordinate demands on himself and at times despising himself to the point of hatred. But whether I despised them or rated them above myself, I dropped my eyes before almost everybody I came across. I even tried experiments: ‘Shall I be able to bear so-and-so’s eyes upon me?’ – and I was always the first to lower my eyes. This drove me to frenzy. I was also morbidly afraid of being ridiculous and therefore slavishly observed the ordinary conventions in every outward appearance; I enthusiastically followed the common rut and was terrified of developing any eccentricity. But how was I to keep it up? I was painfully advanced, as a man of our time should be. But they were dullards, as like one another as a flock of sheep. Perhaps I was the only one in the whole office who always seemed to myself a slave and a coward precisely because I also seemed (to myself) civilized. But I not only seemed, I really was a coward and a slave. I say this without shame. Every decent man in this age is, and must be, a coward and a slave. That is his normal condition. I am profoundly certain of this. That is how he is made and what he was made for. And not only at the present time, because of what may be accidental circumstances, but at all times, a decent man must be a coward and a slave. This is a law of the nature of all decent people on the earth. Even if one of them puts on a show of bravery before somebody else, he ought not to take comfort from that or let himself be carried away by it: he is only showing off to the other person. This is the only and the eternal solution. Only donkeys and mules make a show of bravery, and they only to a limited extent. It is not worth while paying any attention to them, because they count for exactly nothing.

As a result, his character represented an ultimate state of inactivity; he made no effort to accomplish anything, initiate change, or express regret.

I had a complex and often contradictory relationship with my coworkers in the office. On the one hand, I hated them all and felt a strong sense of disdain, but on the other hand, I was also intimidated by them. This led to a pattern of vacillating between feeling superior and inferior to them. I was particularly prone to feelings of self-directed contempt, which I believe is a natural part of being a well-adjusted and critically thinking person. However, this tendency to be overly self-critical would often manifest in a fear of being ridiculed, leading me to internalize a rigid set of social norms and stick to what was considered "normal" and "acceptable". Despite my surface-level appearance of confidence and composure, I was actually deeply anxious and fearful of not meeting societal expectations. In reality, I was both a coward and a slave to the conventions of the time, and this was not just a temporary state, but a fundamental aspect of being a decent person. I genuinely believe that this is the natural condition of anyone who values decency, that is, being constantly intimidated and fearful of making mistakes or being judged by others. This dynamic is an integral part of the human experience across all time periods, and it's only those who are naive or unrefined who would suggest otherwise.
